verdict_reasoning: FN1 is a secreted glycoprotein that exists in three major pools: soluble plasma dimers, cell-surface-deposited multimers in the extracellular matrix, and a documented cell-surface pool assembled via integrin and other receptor binding. The protein has no intrinsic TM anchor but becomes stably incorporated into extracellular matrix and focal adhesion complexes via non-covalent binding to integrins and other cell-surface receptors, where it remains wash-resistant. This constitutes stable surface attachment via receptor-mediated anchoring. Additionally, FN1 undergoes regulated assembly into specialized ECM structures and has documented dual localization — both soluble and matrix-resident pools. The surface accessibility depends on the cellular context and adhesion state rather than being baseline present as a bona-fide membrane-anchored protein.
predicted_key_uncertainty: Whether integrin-bound FN1 at focal adhesions qualifies as 'wash-resistant stable surface attachment' (supporting contextual) versus 'reversible ECM deposition' (supporting no/secreted_only). FN1 is predominantly an ECM / stroma protein rather than a cell-surface plasma-membrane protein; the surface pool depends on active cell adhesion and integrin engagement. Binder accessibility is context-dependent on adhesion state and integrin expression.
